title: Sansad
tagline: Indian Election Strategy Game — 2024 Lok Sabha
summary: A browser-based turn-based political simulation built around the 2024 Indian General Election. Pick a real party — BJP, Congress, AAP, TMC, SP, and more — and fight your way to 272 seats across 7 turns of campaign decisions. Every action has a cost, alliances are fragile, and an AI advisor comments on your strategy turn by turn.

sections.heading: What Is This?
sections.body:
Sansad is a political simulation game built around the 2024 Indian General Election. Pick a real party and fight your way to 272 seats across 7 turns of campaign decisions. Every move costs something, alliances fall apart, and an AI advisor comments on your position turn by turn.

Solo side project, no party affiliation. Started it because I couldn't find a game that takes Indian electoral mechanics seriously — not as a theme, but as the actual system. The Civilization comparison holds: same resource management and coalition logic, but built on real 2024 Lok Sabha data.

Source is currently private while the game is in development. Release planned.

sections.heading: Gameplay Overview
steps: Party Selection — Choose from all major real Indian parties with their actual 2024 seat counts, alliance blocs, ideologies, and regional strongholds. Three difficulty levels: Simple, Realistic, Complex.
Pre-Campaign Setup — Solo mode: pick exactly which of the 543 Lok Sabha seats to contest. Alliance mode: form coalitions with other parties; seat-sharing negotiations run round by round.

Candidate System — Over 5,000 candidates across all 543 constituencies, each with 30+ attributes including popularity, loyalty, betrayal risk, ground network, and incumbency. Denied candidates defect — switching parties, going independent, or demanding alternate seats.
Campaign Launch — A Flash News modal fires at campaign start with categorised breaking headlines: party switches, rebels, ticket denials, new alliances.

7-Turn Campaign — Full interactive India map with all 543 constituency polygons. 12 action types: rallies, sabotage, caste outreach, bribery, candidate poaching, and more. An AI advisor lives in the right panel, commenting on your position each turn.
Alliance Management — The Alliance HQ panel shows coalition composition, majority progress, and affinity indicators. If tension gets too high, the coalition collapses and rebels form new fronts.
End Game — Win condition: 272 seats out of 543. The result screen shows a full declared-seats ticker, alliance bloc breakdown, and final seat map.

screenshots.caption: Party Selection — choose a real party with actual 2024 seat counts, alliances, and regional strongholds
Campaign Map — full interactive India map with all 543 constituency polygons and campaign action panel
Candidate System — AI assigns candidates per constituency; you lock strong picks and swap weaker ones
Alliance HQ — coalition composition, majority progress tracker, and affinity indicators
End Game — full declared-seats ticker, alliance bloc breakdown, and final seat map

sections.heading: Design Challenges
sections.body: Building a politically accurate simulation at this scale surfaces some genuinely hard design problems:
problems: Balancing the vote-share math so that all 12 campaign action types feel meaningfully different and not dominated by a single strategy.
Modelling caste, urban/rural, income, and generational voter blocs accurately without the simulation becoming opaque.
Alliance dynamics: how fragile should coalitions be? At what point does the AI advisor start recommending breaking alliances rather than holding them?
Scalability of the candidate system — tracking 5,000+ candidate states across 7 turns without performance degradation.
Making the game mechanically interesting to players who know nothing about Indian politics while staying authentic enough for those who do.

sections.heading: Interested in Playtesting or Contributing?
sections.body: The source is currently private while the game is in active development. That will change. In the meantime — if you want to playtest, suggest a mechanic, report a bug you spotted in these screenshots, or contribute data improvements:
backgrounds: Data accuracy — candidate attributes, constituency demographics, party affinity scores
New scripted scenarios based on real election events
